incident_records.description: Mayor Ishii's office hours policy explicitly excludes organized groups — only individual constituents may attend. This is not a minor procedural detail: it structurally prevents neighbors with shared concerns from presenting collectively, which is the form of constituent engagement most likely to produce accountability. A group of residents raising a common grievance creates a record, demands a coherent answer, and is harder to dismiss than a series of isolated one-on-ones. Blocking group access is a signal of conflict avoidance, not genuine engagement. For contrast: the mayor of San Francisco maintains a visible daily public presence (business callouts, problem identification, follow-through) that this office does not approach.
incident_records.source: Direct observation by scorecard author; Ishii constituent communications and office hours scheduling policy

incident_records.description: Participated in a public dressing-down of ODPA Director Hansel Aguilar after he used his charter authority to force two items onto the September 2025 council agenda. Aguilar's charter office was created by 85% of voters specifically to have independent authority over the police accountability agenda. Using that authority is not an abuse of power — it is the design. Penalizing Aguilar for exercising the authority voters granted him is either a failure to understand the charter or a deliberate attempt to subordinate the oversight body to council approval. In either case, the effect is to signal to future oversight directors that exercising charter independence will be met with political retaliation.
incident_records.source: Berkeleyside, Alex N. Gecan, Feb. 9, 2026

incident_records.description: Confirmed Aguilar's dismissal after unanimous 8-0 vote (Kesarwani absent) then issued a prepared statement that the council 'remained committed to police accountability' and was focused on 'restoring credibility, trust and respect for both the accountability process and the people involved in this work.' The council had just voted to fire the accountability director, leaving the Police Accountability Board at 4 of 9 seats with all original commissioners departed, and BPD in an ongoing records dispute with the office. Issuing a recommitment-to-accountability statement immediately after eliminating the accountability office's leadership is the definitional form of performative governance: the statement replaces the action it purports to describe.
incident_records.source: Berkeleyside, Alex N. Gecan, Feb. 9, 2026

incident_records.description: Authored 'Advanced Fiscal Policies — Bond Schedule' referral directing City Manager to develop a regular predictable bond issuance schedule. Explicit 'we are underbonded' philosophy: preference to borrow more rather than improve efficiency or cut spending.
incident_records.source: Agenda record 2025-11-18 consent item 26

incident_records.description: Led $300M GO bond survey direction at special session — framing potential new taxpayer debt obligations as infrastructure investment without any corresponding efficiency or cut analysis.
incident_records.source: Agenda record 2025-12-02 special meeting

incident_records.description: Supported sales tax poll referral (Kesarwani) — part of ongoing pattern of reaching for new revenue (bonds, taxes) without first asking what can be cut or done more efficiently.
incident_records.source: Agenda record 2026-01-27 consent item 17

incident_records.description: Distributed constituent newsletter promoting a city-launched 0.5% sales tax community survey to address Berkeley's structural deficit. The survey asks residents what services they want funded — not what they would cut. No mention of reprioritization, efficiency review, or spending reductions as alternatives. Berkeley's structural deficit is a spending problem; a survey that only surfaces new revenue preferences is political infrastructure for a tax campaign, not fiscal analysis.
incident_records.source: March 2026 District 2 Newsletter (ttaplin@cityofberkeley.info)

incident_records.description: March 2026 newsletter promoted community input meetings for a potential 2026 infrastructure bond measure, advertising bond as the mechanism for addressing Berkeley's street and facility needs. Context: Berkeley's streets rank among the worst in Alameda County not from lack of bond authority but from chronic underfunding — approximately $2–8M of the general fund is allocated annually toward paving. The Five Year Paving Plan selects streets based on political considerations rather than efficiency-maximizing pavement management metrics. Framing infrastructure need as a bonding opportunity rather than a general fund reprioritization problem obscures the actual cause of decline and compounds taxpayer debt burden.
incident_records.source: March 2026 District 2 Newsletter (ttaplin@cityofberkeley.info); Five Year Paving Plan update presented February 4, 2026

incident_records.description: Present and voted to 'receive and file' the City Auditor's 'Rocky Road' streets audit (Item 35), which documented PCI 57, a $42M/year funding gap, and a structural pattern of bond dependence that crowded out general fund reprioritization. No follow-up motion to reform the Five Year Paving Plan or redirect general fund dollars was ever introduced. Five months later (March 2026), Taplin and the full council unanimously directed staff to develop a $300M infrastructure bond for the November 2026 ballot — the fifth bond cycle addressing streets since 2012 — without any companion motion to address the strategic failures the audit documented. The bond-over-reprioritization choice was made with eyes open.
incident_records.source: Agenda record 2025-10-28 Item 35 (Rocky Road audit, 'Rocky Road: Berkeley Streets at Risk and Significantly Underfunded'); agenda record 2026-03-17 Item 1

incident_records.description: Effective and sustained advocate for deploying Automated License Plate Readers (ALPRs) in Berkeley — a data-driven public safety tool that improves police effectiveness at low ongoing cost. Taplin navigated significant political resistance and delivered a concrete result. This is the clearest example in the scorecard period of a member identifying a core-service improvement, doing the work to advance it, and seeing it through. It stands in contrast to the prevailing council pattern of performative process and borrowed credit.
incident_records.source: Direct knowledge of scorecard author; public record of ALPR policy development

incident_records.description: Authored 'Budget Referral and Updated Guidelines and Procedures for City Council Office Staff Expenditures' — passed on the consent calendar — doubling authorized council office staffing from 1 FTE to 2 FTE per office. Immediate budget referral: up to $219,080 (AAO process). Full annual cost at 2 FTE per office: approximately $442,000 above prior baseline, recurring. Co-authors: Kate Harrison and Ben Bartlett. Three compounding problems: (1) A recurring $442k/year expenditure passed on the consent calendar during a period of acknowledged structural deficit — no floor debate, no alternatives analysis, no cost-benefit framing relative to competing priorities. (2) More council office staff does not reduce the structural deficit; it increases the ongoing cost of governance and — as the output of unfocused activity historically demonstrates — tends to increase the volume of non-core agenda items rather than their quality. (3) The item explicitly references that 'half of Council Offices now maintain more than one Legislative Assistant' as justification for funding that which already existed informally — institutionalizing a practice rather than questioning whether it was appropriate in the first place.
incident_records.source: Agenda item Nov. 16, 2023, Item 3 (consent calendar); Taplin as author, Harrison (subsequently resigned) and Bartlett co-authors

incident_records.description: Taplin authored a 'Advanced Fiscal Policies — Bond Schedule' referral directing the City Manager to develop a regular predictable bond issuance schedule — demonstrating detailed engagement with the mechanics of municipal borrowing. Yet Berkeley's council fiscal policies have never required assessing whether recurring revenues match recurring expenditures — a foundational GFOA best practice that applies to any government serious about structural balance. This is not a new standard: GFOA has published it for decades. Taplin invested authorship capital in optimizing bond-issuance cadence while never authoring anything that would test whether Berkeley should be borrowing at all. The asymmetry reveals a disposition: the borrowing machinery gets attention; the structural balance test does not.
incident_records.source: Agenda record 2025-11-18 consent item 26; GFOA best practice on Debt Management Policy; City Auditor report 'Berkeley's Financial Condition (FY2016–FY2025),' April 2026 (documents the missing policy as a finding)

incident_records.description: Backed creation of the African American Holistic Resource Center — a new building and staffing for a statistically small share of the population, driven by activist claims of underservice. No measurement of whether existing services were adequate, no alternatives analysis, no performance metrics required. Classic pattern: demographic grievance → new institution, bypassing any efficiency review of the status quo.
incident_records.source: Direct knowledge of scorecard author

incident_records.description: AAHRC: no effort made to determine whether existing city services could be reformed, consolidated, or redirected to meet the stated need. The question 'what do we already have and is it working?' was never asked publicly.
incident_records.source: Direct knowledge of scorecard author

incident_records.description: Documented pattern of requesting co-author credit on agenda items after the primary authorship work is already complete. Bartlett attaches his name to proposals developed by others — gaining the political visibility of authorship without having shaped the item's analysis, framing, or fiscal structure. Co-authorship in this context is a credit-seeking signal, not a substantive contribution. It inflates the appearance of legislative engagement while the work — and accountability for it — belongs to the actual authors.
incident_records.source: Direct observation; sourced from council colleagues familiar with his working pattern

incident_records.description: Scheduled community meeting on the Howard Johnson Motel interim housing project for December 11, 2024 — three weeks after the City Council had already voted on November 19, 2024 to approve the contract with Dorothy Day House to operate it. The invitation email acknowledged the vote had occurred ('By way of background, at its November 19 meeting, the City Council approved a contract...') while framing the December meeting as a community conversation. This was post-facto notification dressed as engagement.
incident_records.source: Tregub constituent email Dec 11 2024 (ITregub@berkeleyca.gov); agenda record 2024-11-19

incident_records.description: Told constituent there was 'no other good use' for the homeless hotel real estate — dismissed alternatives without any analysis, market study, or public process.
incident_records.source: Direct constituent interaction with scorecard author

incident_records.description: Claimed not to know about the homeless hotel project despite it having been in the city staff pipeline for approximately 6 months and located in his own district.
incident_records.source: Direct constituent interaction with scorecard author

incident_records.description: March 2026 (Part 2) newsletter told constituents that passage of both a $300M infrastructure bond AND a half-cent sales tax was 'a sobering fact...essential to ensure the city's continued ability to deliver on the quality services that you deserve.' This is revenue-first framing to constituents with no mention of reprioritization or efficiency alternatives. The bond and tax are being prepared for the November 2026 ballot — continuing an unbroken cycle of bond measures (Measure M 2012, T1 2016, L failed 2022, EE/FF 2024) with no corresponding general fund reprioritization. At the same time, Tregub voted unanimously with the rest of the council to commission a second poll on both measures.
incident_records.source: Tregub March 2026 Part 2 newsletter (igor@igortregub.com, 2026-03-18)

incident_records.description: Used official District 4 constituent newsletter to mobilize residents to attend an Alameda County Board of Supervisors meeting in support of Measure W homelessness funding — an active lobbying call on behalf of county-level spending. Three compounding problems: (1) City and county measures are additive obligations on the same taxpayer base; drawing constituent attention to county spending campaigns is unusual precisely because it highlights cumulative burden — members typically avoid doing so. (2) Measure W would direct county revenue preferentially to cities that implement Housing First programs — i.e., Berkeley — meaning Tregub was effectively campaigning to route county money into the same Berkeley HSO apparatus whose outcomes he has never publicly questioned. (3) Lobbying for a county measure is outside a city councilmember's jurisdiction; using an official constituent email list for this purpose treats that list as an advocacy tool rather than a public communication channel.
incident_records.source: July 2025 District 4 Newsletter (igor@igortregub.com)

incident_records.description: Present and voted to 'receive and file' the City Auditor's 'Rocky Road' streets audit (Item 35), which documented PCI 57, a $42M/year funding gap, and a structural pattern of bond dependence that crowded out general fund reprioritization. No follow-up motion to reform the Five Year Paving Plan or redirect general fund dollars was introduced. Five months later (March 2026), Tregub voted unanimously with the council to commission a $300M infrastructure bond survey, and his March 2026 newsletter described the bond as 'essential' — without mentioning the audit's finding that the underlying problem is chronic underfunding of paving in the general fund, not lack of bond authority. The audit was received, filed, and converted into a bond campaign.
incident_records.source: Agenda record 2025-10-28 Item 35 (Rocky Road audit); agenda record 2026-03-17 Item 1; Tregub March 2026 Part 2 newsletter
